definition: Inspiring awe or reverence; worthy of profound respect; majestic.
confidence_reason: Attested in Old English religious usage with positive connotation of divine majesty.
citation: OED 2nd ed., awful adj. historical senses.

definition: Causing great fear or dread; terrifying; dreadful.
confidence_reason: Documented in Middle English with strong negative emotional force.
reconstruction_level: Shift from positive awe to fearful dread.

definition: Very bad or unpleasant; of poor quality.
confidence_reason: Widely attested in nineteenth-century colloquial usage.
citation: OED 2nd ed., awful adj. colloquial sense.
reconstruction_level: Loss of original intensity through semantic bleaching.

confidence_reason: Earliest English sense is pejorative and historically tied to Latin nescius through Old French.
reconstruction_level: Origin: Latin nescius via Old French nice (foolish).

confidence_reason: Intermediate stage between foolish and pleasant; used in technical and scholarly contexts.
citation: OED 2nd ed., nice adj. precise/exacting senses.

confidence_reason: Now the dominant sense in most registers.
citation: OED 2nd ed., nice adj. pleasant sense.
reconstruction_level: One of the most documented cases of amelioration in English.

definition: To come before; to precede; to anticipate.
confidence_reason: The original English sense follows Latin praevenire as priority rather than obstruction.
reconstruction_level: Calque of Latin praevenire. Now an opaque etymological sense.

definition: To hinder or obstruct; to keep from happening.
confidence_reason: The semantic shift from preceding to obstructing is complete in Early Modern English.
citation: OED 2nd ed., prevent v. hinder sense.

definition: To stop something from happening; to avert.
confidence_reason: The universal modern sense is well documented in modern dictionaries and historical dictionaries.
citation: OED 2nd ed., prevent v. modern sense.

definition: To carry; to bear (a burden or load).
confidence_reason: Latin portare is an etymological source form, not an attested English period meaning.
reconstruction_level: Root of import export transport portable: all trace to Latin portare.

confidence_reason: From naught (nothing) plus -y. Original sense concerned poverty or worthlessness rather than mild misbehavior.
reconstruction_level: Striking pejoration: poverty to moral badness.

confidence_reason: The moral sense displaces the poverty sense in Early Modern English.
citation: OED 2nd ed., naughty adj. wicked sense.

confidence_reason: Further bleaching from wicked to merely mischievous.
citation: OED 2nd ed., naughty adj. mild/mischievous sense.
reconstruction_level: Classic three-stage drift: poverty to evil to mischievous.

confidence_reason: From Old English saelig (blessed); cognate with German selig.
citation: OED 2nd ed., silly adj. early blessed/happy senses.

definition: Innocent; helpless; pitiable; deserving of compassion.
confidence_reason: Shift from blessed to innocent remains positive or neutral.
Connotation turns negative as helplessness becomes foolishness.
citation: OED 2nd ed., silly adj. simple/weak senses.

confidence_reason: The modern universal sense is well documented.
citation: OED 2nd ed., silly adj. foolish sense.
reconstruction_level: Complete journey from blessed to foolish in about five centuries.
